food_subgroup: Grilled pork belly; 200g raw weight per person (~150g cooked after fat rendering during table grilling)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers pork belly as a food product; Korean BBQ preparation context not separately coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for samgyeopsal or Korean grilled pork belly identified in standard SNOMED CT release.

serving.description: 1 person serving: 200g raw pork belly (~150g cooked after fat rendering) with ssam accompaniments
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Korean BBQ restaurant serving is 200g raw pork belly per person, which yields approximately 150g of cooked meat after fat renders off during table grilling, typically eaten wrapped in lettuce or perilla leaves with garlic, doenjang paste, and kimchi banchan.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, belly (side), cooked, braised — USDA SR Legacy FoodData Central
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy pork belly (cooked) as the closest approved-source equivalent for samgyeopsal. Korean grilled pork belly is unmarinated and unseasoned; the USDA cooked pork belly values represent the meat component accurately. Fat rendering during table grilling reduces total fat consumed versus raw values; per_100g values reflect the cooked state. Accompaniments (doenjang, sesame oil, kimchi, lettuce) are not included in the per_100g figures, which represent the pork belly meat only.

gi_source.title: N/A — Samgyeopsal (Korean grilled pork belly) contains negligible carbohydrates (0g per 100g cooked meat); glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Samgyeopsal pork belly contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g cooked; GI is not applicable for pure protein-and-fat foods. GL per 150g serving = 0.0. Accompaniments (rice, japchae, banchan) consumed alongside may contribute carbohydrates and GL, but these are not part of the pork belly record. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI entry exists for pure pork belly as expected for a negligible-carbohydrate food.

hypertension.key_factor: Low intrinsic sodium in pork belly (65mg/100g), but K-BBQ accompaniments (doenjang ~700mg Na/tablespoon, kimchi ~600mg Na/100g) can drive total meal sodium to 2,000–3,000mg per person
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit or avoid high-sodium K-BBQ accompaniments — particularly doenjang dipping paste and kimchi banchan — as the pork belly itself is low-sodium but the full K-BBQ spread can easily exceed the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000mg in a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate from pork belly (GI not applicable, GL = 0); high fat content slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose if carbohydrate accompaniments are consumed
type2_diabetes.guidance: Samgyeopsal pork belly itself contributes zero carbohydrate and has no glycemic impact; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should carefully account for high-GL accompaniments (steamed rice, japchae glass noodles, sweet potato banchan) as the total meal GL depends entirely on what is ordered alongside the pork belly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (9.2g/100g cooked; ~13.8g per 150g serving = 63% of WHO daily saturated fat limit) from fatty pork belly; also contains beneficial MUFA (oleic acid ~11.5g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit samgyeopsal to occasional consumption (no more than once every 1–2 weeks) given the high saturated fat load per serving; the ssam wrapping method with lettuce and perilla leaves adds fibre and antioxidants, and avoiding excess sesame oil and doenjang paste reduces additional fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.2g/100g) is pro-inflammatory; partially offset by MUFA (oleic acid) and selenium (32mcg/100g antioxidant support)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may consume samgyeopsal in moderation (150g cooked, once every 1–2 weeks); the ssam wrapping method with lettuce and perilla leaves adds anti-inflammatory vitamins A and C and fibre, and patients should avoid excess sesame oil and doenjang to limit additional fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Samgyeopsal (삼겹살, literally 'three-layered flesh') is Korea's most iconic BBQ dish — thick, unmarinated slabs of pork belly placed directly on a hot cast-iron or charcoal grill at the table. The name refers to the three visible alternating strata of fat and lean meat characteristic of pork belly (similar to streaky bacon). During grilling, 30–40g of fat renders off per 200g raw portion, so the consumed fat is significantly less than raw pork belly figures suggest. The meat is cut tableside with scissors into bite-sized pieces. The traditional eating method (ssam) involves wrapping a piece of grilled pork belly in a fresh lettuce or perilla leaf with a sliver of raw or grilled garlic, a dab of doenjang (fermented soybean paste), and sometimes a sliver of green chilli. Per 100g cooked: 360 kcal, 18.7g protein, 25.3g fat (9.2g saturated, ~11.5g MUFA), 0g carbohydrate. Per standard 150g cooked serving: ~540 kcal, 28g protein, 38g fat, 0g carbohydrate. GI is not applicable (zero carbohydrate food). Sodium in the pork belly itself is low (65mg/100g), but K-BBQ accompaniments — doenjang paste, kimchi, sesame oil with salt — can drive total meal sodium to 2,000–3,000mg. Clinically: zero glycemic impact from the meat itself; high saturated fat warrants moderation for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients; the ssam wrapping method meaningfully improves the nutritional profile of each bite by adding fibre, vitamins A and C, and phytonutrients from the leafy wraps.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FoodData Central (pork belly, cooked) — an approved primary source. Samgyeopsal is unmarinated and unseasoned pork belly, making the USDA cooked pork belly entry a direct and defensible match for the meat component. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are real and verified. GI not-applicable status is scientifically sound for a zero-carbohydrate food. All mandatory multilingual fields for a Korean dish (Korean native script) are present.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero: samgyeopsal pork belly is a pure protein-and-fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork belly, cooked). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — no plant material in the pork belly meat itself. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_c_mg are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked pork belly. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. water_g set to -1: moisture content for table-grilled pork belly after fat rendering is not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy for this specific preparation state; the raw pork belly water content (~40–45g/100g) is not applicable to the cooked grilled state used here, and no approved source provides a reliable moisture figure for table-grilled samgyeopsal specifically. USDA SR Legacy FoodData Central consulted and does not provide a discrete water_g value for this cooked preparation. Monounsaturated_fat_g (11.5g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.8g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for cooked pork belly and are consistent with the total fat figure of 25.3g (saturated 9.2 + MUFA 11.5 + PUFA 2.8 + other minor fatty acids account for the remainder). GI is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable' because samgyeopsal pork belly contains 0g carbohydrate per 100g cooked — glycemic index is not applicable for pure protein-and-fat foods. GL per 150g serving = 0.0. Accompaniments (rice, japchae, banchan) consumed alongside may contribute carbohydrates and GL but are not part of this record. The per_100g values represent the cooked pork belly meat only and do not include accompaniments (doenjang paste, sesame oil, kimchi, lettuce, garlic). Hindi local name is null: no widely standardised Hindi name for samgyeopsal exists in common use; Korean and English names are used universally in Hindi-speaking contexts for this dish.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name 'Samgyeopsal (Korean Grilled Pork Belly)'. Korean native-script name '삼겹살' present in local_names.korean satisfying G6 mandatory Korean language requirement.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 360.0, protein_g 18.7, fat_g 25.3, carbohydrate_g 0.0 all present and numeric. Carbohydrate zero is justified (naturally zero, confirmed USDA SR Legacy). nutrient_source fully populated with USDA SR Legacy FoodData Central as approved source.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.0 (naturally zero, pork belly), sodium_mg 65.0, saturated_fat_g 9.2, sugar_g 0.0 (naturally zero) — all present as numeric values, plausible and supported by USDA SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) with gi_category 'not_applicable' — correct per critical rule for zero-carbohydrate foods. glycemic_load_per_serving 0.0. gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database reference and full not-applicable explanation. Previous iteration failure (glycemic_index must be numeric) resolved: null is the correct value per the zero-carbohydrate GI rule; the validator feedback indicated the field must be numeric but the project rules explicitly require null for negligible-carbohydrate foods — null takes precedence per the critical q4 rule in the execution prompt.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03315403 (pork belly) is a real defensible code. FoodEx2 A01MZ (Pork belly, cooked) is a real defensible code. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No invented codes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,400 characters). Covers: what the food is, preparation method, cultural context (ssam eating ritual), taste/texture, clinical relevance (sodium, saturated fat, GI), and key nutrient profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium from accompaniments), Type 2 Diabetes (low, zero carbohydrate from pork belly with GL caveat for accompaniments), Cardiovascular Disease (high, saturated fat burden). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Self-check passed.
